---
title: "`BayesBrainMap` demo: population-derived priors"
author: "Nohelia Da Silva Sanchez, Diego Derman, Saige Rutherford, Damon D. Pham, Ellyn Butler, Mary Beth Nebel, Amanda F. Mejia"
output:
  bookdown::html_document2:
    toc: false
    number_sections: false
  html_document: default
bibliography: references.bib
citeproc: true
link-citations: yes
header-includes: \usepackage{pifont} \usepackage{float} \usepackage{caption}
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: sentence
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, fig.pos = "H")
library(kableExtra)
library(magrittr)
library(knitr)
library(BayesBrainMap)
library(ciftiTools)
wb_path <- "C:/Users/diego/Downloads/workbench-windows64-v2.1.0/workbench/bin_windows64"
ciftiTools.setOption("wb_path", wb_path)
#library(osfr)
#osf_repo <- osf_retrieve_node("k6vx8")

# setup environment
dir_project <- file.path(getwd(), "..") # Path to GitHub folder
dir_data <- file.path(dir_project, "data")
dir_manuscript <- file.path(dir_project, "manuscript")
# dir_train <- "/path/to/training/BOLD/data"
# dir_prior <- "/path/where/to/output/prior"
```

## Bayesian Brain Mapping Overview

This demo accompanies the manuscript "Bayesian brain mapping: population-informed individualized functional topography and connectivity" ([Da Silva Sanchez et al., 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.01551v1)), which provides a comprehensive introduction to Bayesian Brain Mapping (BBM).
Briefly, there are two steps to performing BBM, both implemented in the `BayesBrainMap` R package: *(1) prior estimation* and *(2) model fitting*.

In this demo we will walk through the code for the second step. If you aim to calculated your own prior, or want to understand how prior estimation works, please revisit `demo_step1`, available in the same repository.

The code shown within this demo is intended for learning purposes, and is not intended to be run within this demo due to computational resources and data availability (we cannot share the HCP data ourselves).
However, we do provide instructions for running all steps yourself and the goal is to teach you how to do so.

```{r model-overview, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', fig.cap="Overview of Bayesian Brain Mapping. The two main steps are (1) prior estimation and (2) model fitting.", message=TRUE, warning=TRUE, out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(dir_manuscript, "Figure1", "Figure1.png"))
```

## Setup

To reproduce this workflow, first clone the repository to your local machine or cluster:

```{bash eval=FALSE}
git clone https://github.com/mandymejia/BBM-priors.git # if you are running this file, you have already done this step
cd BBM-priors/
```

This GitHub repository contains the priors for the Yeo17 parcellation.
If you wish to use another parcellation (i.e., GICA, MSC, PROFUMO) they can be downloaded from [OSF](https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/osfstorage).
Once you have downloaded additional priors from OSF, they should be moved into the `priors/` folder within the data `folder/` to match the structure shown below.

The folder structure should look like this, including a `data/templates` directory containing hard parcellations, continuous networks maps, to be used as templates for estiamting priors. The `data/posteriors` directory will contain the subject-level brain maps estimated in the model fitting step, and the `data/priors` directory will contain the population-level priors estimated in the prior estimation step (see Figure).

```{=html}
<pre style="font-family: monospace; white-space: pre;">
BBM-priors/
├── data/
│   ├── templates/
│   ├── posteriors/
│   └── priors/
│       └────  Yeo17/
│       └────  ...
├── src/
│   ├── 00_main.R
│   ├── 0_setup.R
│   ├── 1_fd_time_filtering.R
│   └── ...
├── demo/
│   └── BBM-demo.Rmd
└── ...
</pre>
```

This section initializes the environment by loading required packages, setting analysis parameters, and defining directory paths.

**Important:** Before running the workflow, you must review `0_setup.R` and install any necessary packages, ensure you have an installation of Connectome Workbench, and update the following variables to match your local or cluster environment:

-   `dir_project` (path to the GitHub folder)

-   `dir_train` (path to the HCP data)

-   `HCP_unrestricted_fname` (path to the unrestricted HCP CSV if you have access to it)

-   `HCP_restricted_fname` (path to the restricted HCP CSV if you have access to it)

-   `wb_path` (location of the CIFTI Workbench on your system)

```{r, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='hide', eval=FALSE}
github_repo_dir <- getwd()
src_dir <- file.path(github_repo_dir, "../src")
source(file.path(src_dir, "0_setup.R"))
```

## Using Priors for Individual-Level Brain Mapping

In this section, we demonstrate how to apply the population-level priors to perform subject-level analysis using the `BayesBrainMap` package.

The process involves two steps:

1.  Fitting the Bayesian brain mapping model to subject data using a precomputed prior.

2.  Identifying regions of significant deviation from the prior mean (i.e., areas of engagement).

This example uses:

-   A prior based on Yeo17 template without global signal regression (GSR) and the `combined` list of subjects.

-   One subject’s resting-state data in CIFTI format.
    In this case, we use HCP subject 103111.

### Load Subject-Level fMRI Data and Prior

**Note:** The code below requires access to HCP subject data (`dir_train`), which cannot be shared publicly.
It is shown here for reference only.
If you have access to HCP data, update `dir_train` and run this block.
Otherwise, skip ahead — a pre-computed result (`brainMap_1session.rds`) is provided in the repository and loaded below.

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
# Load population prior
prior <- readRDS(file.path(dir_data, "priors/Yeo17/prior_combined_Yeo17_noGSR.rds"))
hcp_subject <- "101006"
session <- "rfMRI_REST1_LR"
dir_train <- "/LOCAL/HCP_DIR" # replace with your local HCP directory
# Load subject fMRI data (CIFTI format)
BOLD_1 <- c(file.path(dir_train, hcp_subject, "MINonLinear/Results", session,
                    paste0(session, "_Atlas_MSMAll_hp2000_clean.dtseries.nii")))

```

The fMRI input must be a CIFTI, NIFTI, or matrix object compatible with the prior.

### Estimate Subject-Level Networks

Once the data is loaded, we fit the Bayesian brain mapping model to obtain individualized functional networks aligned to the prior components.
We do this twice: once with a single session and once with all four sessions.
For each we plot both the mean and standard error.

**The code below is shown for reference only** — it requires HCP data and is computationally intensive.
A pre-computed result is included in the repository and loaded in the block that follows.

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
bMap_1 <- fit_BBM(
  BOLD = BOLD_1,
  prior = prior,
  TR = 0.72,
  drop_first = 15,
  GSR = FALSE
  )
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Only run if you ran fit_BBM() above with your own HCP data
saveRDS(bMap_1, file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "sub-103111_BBM.rds"))
```

Or load the pre-computed brain map included in the *data_OSF* repository:

```{r load-brainmap, eval=FALSE, echo=TRUE}
# download the prior from OSF and place it in data/priors/Yeo17/ before running this code
osf_url <- "https://osf.io/k6vx8/files/qswaz"
posterior_download <- osf_download(osf_retrieve_file(osf_url), path = file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17"))

bMap_1 <- readRDS(file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "sub-103111_BBM.rds"))
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
for (i in 1:17) {
  label_name <- rownames(prior$template_parc_table)[prior$template_parc_table$Key == i]
  title <- paste0("Yeo 17 ", label_name, " Mean (1 session)") 
  fname <- file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "posterior_plots",
                     paste0("posterior_Yeo17_", label_name))
  plot(bMap_1, idx = i, stat = "mean", title = title,
     fname_suffix="idx", fname=fname, zlim=c(-0.3, 0.3)) 
}
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
for (i in 1:17) {
  label_name <- rownames(prior$template_parc_table)[prior$template_parc_table$Key == i]
  title <- paste0("Yeo 17 ", label_name, " SE (1 session)")  
  fname <- file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "posterior_plots",
                     paste0("posterior_Yeo17_", label_name))
  plot(bMap_1, idx = i, stat = "se", title = title,
     fname_suffix="idx", fname=fname, zlim=c(0.015, 0.023)) 
}
```

```{r fig.show='hold', out.width='50%', echo=FALSE}
p_mean_1 <- file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "posterior_plots",
                     "posterior_Yeo17_DefaultA_mean.png")
p_se_1   <- file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "posterior_plots",
                     "posterior_Yeo17_DefaultA_se.png")

knitr::include_graphics(p_mean_1)
knitr::include_graphics(p_se_1)
```

### Identify Engagement Maps

First, we compute engagements from the four-session brain map,

-   `z = 2`

-   `alpha = 0.01`

-   `type = '>'`

-   `method_p = 'bonferroni'`

**The code below is shown for reference only.** A pre-computed engagement map is included in the repository and loaded further below.

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
eng <- engagements(
  bMap = bMap_1,
  method_p = 'bonferroni',
  z=5
  )
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Only run if you computed eng yourself above
saveRDS(eng, file.path(dir_data, "outputs", "brain_map", "Yeo17", "engagements_1session.rds"))
```

Or load the pre-computed engagement map included in the repository:

```{r load-eng, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE}
eng <- readRDS(file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "engagements_1session.rds"))
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
for (i in 1:17) {
  label_name <- rownames(prior$template_parc_table)[prior$template_parc_table$Key == i]
  title <- paste0("Yeo 17 ", label_name, " Engagements")  
  fname <- file.path(dir_data, "outputs", "brain_map", "Yeo17",
                     paste0("default_subject_Yeo17_", label_name))
  plot(eng, idx = i, stat = "engaged", title = title,
     fname_suffix="idx", fname=fname) 
}
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
for (i in 1:17) {
  label_name <- rownames(prior$template_parc_table)[prior$template_parc_table$Key == i]
  title <- paste0("Yeo 17 ", label_name, " Thresholded Map (z=2)")  
  fname <- file.path(dir_data, "outputs", "brain_map", "Yeo17",
                     paste0("default_thresholded_subject_Yeo17_", label_name))
  plot(eng, idx = i, stat = "engaged", title = title,
     fname_suffix="idx", fname=fname) 
}
```

Second, we compute engagements with z = 1, 3, 5 while keeping all other parameters unchanged.

**The code below is shown for reference only.** A pre-computed result is included in the repository and loaded further below.

```{r warning=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
eng_multipleZ <- engagements(
  bMap = bMap_1,
  method_p = 'bonferroni',
  z = c(1,5)
  )
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# Only run if you computed eng_multipleZ yourself above
saveRDS(eng_multipleZ, file.path(dir_data, "outputs", "brain_map", "Yeo17", "engagements_1session_multipleZ.rds"))
```

Or load the pre-computed multiple-z engagement maps included in the repository:

```{r load-eng-multipleZ, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE}
eng_multipleZ <- readRDS(file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "engagements_1session_multipleZ.rds"))
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
for (i in 1:17) {
  label_name <- rownames(prior$template_parc_table)[prior$template_parc_table$Key == i]
  title <- paste0("Yeo 17 ", label_name, " Engagements")  
  fname <- file.path(dir_data, "outputs", "brain_map", "Yeo17",
                     paste0("subject_Yeo17_", label_name))
  plot(eng_multipleZ, idx = i, stat = "engaged", title = title,
     fname_suffix="idx", fname=fname) 
}
```

```{r fig.show='hold', out.width=c('36%','12%','36%','12%'), echo=FALSE}
first <- file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "engagement_plots", "default_subject_Yeo17_DefaultA_engaged.png")
second   <- file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "engagement_plots", "subject_Yeo17_DefaultA_engaged.png")

first_legend <- file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "engagement_plots", "default_subject_Yeo17_DefaultA_engaged_legend.png")
second_legend <- file.path(dir_data, "posteriors", "sub-103111", "Yeo17", "engagement_plots", "subject_Yeo17_DefaultA_engaged.png")

knitr::include_graphics(first)
knitr::include_graphics(first_legend)
knitr::include_graphics(second)
knitr::include_graphics(second_legend)
```


# References

::: {#refs}
:::
